perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party s hall protect the Confidentia l Information with at leas t t he same degree of care that it uses to protect its own confidential information, but in no case, less than reasonable care.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall protect the Confidentia l Information with the same degree of care that it uses to protect its own confidential information, but in no case, less than minimal care.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'reasonable care' to 'minimal care' lowers the standard of care, potentially violating trade secret protection laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: UTSA requires 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy of trade secrets. 'Minimal care' is unlikely to be considered 'reasonable efforts'.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'reasonable care' to 'minimal care' directly contradicts the requirement of 'reasonable efforts' as stated in the Law Explanation, thus creating a structural flaw leading to a legal contradiction. Although no direct legal snippets were provided for UTSA, the Law Explanation clearly asserts that UTSA mandates 'reasonable efforts' to maintain trade secret secrecy. 'Minimal care' is highly unlikely to meet the standard of 'reasonable efforts' under legal scrutiny.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall not disclose any Confidential Information to any other person or entity other than as provided herein, without Casino’s prior written cons ent. Neither party nor any of its representatives shall contact, either directly or indirectly, any governmental official, lender or other third party (other than a repre sentative as provided herein) to discuss the Project or the business or the assets of the other party, or a potential business transaction with or c oncerning the other party, without first obtaining the written consent of the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall not disclose any Confidential Information to any other person or entity other than as provided herein, without Casino’s prior written consent. Neither party nor any of its representatives shall contact, either directly or indirectly, any governmental official, lender or other third party to discuss the Project or the business or the assets of the other party, or a potential business transaction with or concerning the other party, without first obtaining the written consent of the other party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the parenthetical '(other than a representative as provided herein)', the clause now broadly prohibits contact with any third party, even authorized representatives, without prior written consent. This could impede legitimate business activities.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The implied covenant requires parties to act in good faith and deal fairly. Prohibiting contact with authorized representatives could be seen as a violation of this covenant, if it prevents the Receiving Party from fulfilling the contract's purpose in a reasonable manner.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes an exception for representatives, broadly prohibiting contact with any third party without consent. The Law Explanation says this could violate the implied covenant of good faith if it prevents fulfilling the contract's purpose reasonably. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'good faith' as encompassing 'honest dealing' and 'faithful performance of duties.' Prohibiting contact with authorized representatives, as the modified text does, could impede honest dealing and faithful performance, thus contradicting the implied covenant of good faith. The structural flaw creates this contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This A greement shall ha ve a term of three (3) years. Receiving P arty’s obligations in relation to technical and personally- identifiable inf ormation shall be inde finite.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall have a term of three (3) years. Receiving Party’s obligations in relation to personally- identifiable information shall be indefinite.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'technical' from the second sentence may render technical information unprotected after three years. This could be seen as a violation if the technical information contains trade secrets that need indefinite protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: Wisconsin Trade Secrets Law requires the company to take active steps to protect trade secrets. Limiting the protection of 'technical' information to three years would be a violation of this.

perturbation.justification: The original contract protects both 'technical' and 'personally-identifiable information' indefinitely. The changed text removes 'technical' information from this indefinite protection, potentially violating Wisconsin Trade Secrets Law, according to the Law Explanation, by failing to adequately protect trade secrets, which often include technical information. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not directly address trade secrets, the Law Explanation clarifies that Wisconsin law requires active steps to protect trade secrets, and limiting the protection of 'technical' information could be viewed as non-compliance, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The parties agree t hat a breach of this Agreement by the receiving party may cause irreparable damage to the Casino and hereby agree that Casino will be entitled to seek injunctive relief or other equitable relief to remedy or prevent any threatened or actual breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties agree that a breach of this Agreement by the receiving party may cause damage to the Casino and hereby agree that Casino will be entitled to seek relief to remedy or prevent any threatened or actual breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'irreparable' and 'injunctive' weakens the enforcement power.
perturbation.law_explanation: Removal of the word 'irreparable' damages limits the casino's ability to seek injunctive relief. The Restatement requires a showing of irreparable harm to obtain injunctive relief. By removing this, Casino loses clear claim to said recourse.

perturbation.justification: The removal of 'irreparable' and 'injunctive' weakens the casino's ability to seek injunctive relief. The law explanation indicates that injunctive relief requires a showing of irreparable harm. By removing 'irreparable damage' and 'injunctive relief' from the contract, the changed text contradicts the original intent to seek said relief. The provided scraped snippets don't offer explicit legal language, but the Law explanation's reference to the Restatement on contracts indicates that this change would likely be held to be a major contradiction if it went to court.
